# Transcrição: Lesson 5- How to -Move the Free Line-

- **Fonte:** Lesson 5- How to -Move the Free Line-
- **Duração:** 11:47
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Okay, so now I want to talk about, this could be a real paradigm shift for you, depending on where you're coming from. And that's, well, it's actually something that Eben Pagan, a good friend of mine, very accomplished information marketer, he calls moving the free line. Now this is something that I sort of stumbled into many, many, many, in fact, many years ago. In fact, it's something that I literally, I started doing this right from the very beginning because a long story, maybe I'll tell you the stories, we go through this, but then Eben really gave it this great name, he calls it moving the free line. So this is, here's the free line right down here. This is the free line. And basically, let's just say, this line represents what you're going to give away, as opposed to sell. Like what part of your information are you going to give away? And this axis over here is value. And basically what happens is, as you go move up here, you move the free line, you know, because the free line was here and you're giving away, in other words, this much value, which was like some rather minimal amount of value to get people interested in what you're doing. Or maybe you're back over here and you're not giving any value before the sale. And then you can move the free line and move up, and maybe you're giving more value. And then maybe you decide to actually be up here and you're giving away massive value. You're giving away some of your best information, some of your absolute best information that you have. An interesting thing happens as you do this. We're going to draw a line here, and this line is your price. How much you can get people to pay you for your information. And a funny thing happens, as you move up that free line, your price does the same thing. So basically what happens is when you start giving away massive value, you can start charging really high prices. And you know, one of the things that I've always done is I've charged higher prices. I don't want to compete on price. Walmart does a fantastic job of competing on price. They do a great job at that. I don't want to be in the Walmart business because there's always someone that's going to come in and try to undercut you on price, especially online. People are going to try to undercut you and sell for cheaper. If you're selling at $97, then someone's going to come in and try to sell for $77. Then you drop your price to $47, someone's going to come in at $27. You drop your price to $17, someone's going to come in at $7.

## [03:02 - 06:03]

And it's just not a good place to be. You want to be at the higher end and consistently moving higher and higher. And the way you do that is two ways. One is you move the free line and the other way is that you create more and more value. You create more value in your products and raise your prices. And that's the way you want to be going after the information marketing business. You don't want to be at the low end. This is how you get higher prices and you make more money. It's a lot easier. If you're going to make a million bucks, it's a lot easier to sell a thousand people something for $1,000 than it is to sell a million people something for a dollar. It's much, much easier. There's always going to be a certain segment of the market that buys on price. And there's always going to be a certain segment that buys on value. Trust me, your business is going to be a lot better if you're selling on value. One of the things, in addition, that it's easier to make more money because you have to make fewer sales at higher prices that are easier to make. Unless you're Walmart, unless you can create this distribution system that Walmart has and unless you can create the brand that Walmart has, which took them decades to build, you don't want to be there. You don't have decades to build your business and you're not going to be reaching millions and millions of people. You're going to be reaching hundreds or thousands or tens of thousands of people. So it's a lot easier to sell fewer units at a higher price. Another thing is you get better customers. Trust me on this. I've sold, well, actually I've always sold fairly high end. But I've sold different levels of high end and the higher you sell for, the better. The better the customers, the easier the customer service, the better results they're going to have. When people pay you $1,000 for your product or $1,000 instead of 100, or if they pay you 100 instead of 10, the people that pay you more, they are going to be the ones that actually open your product and use your product and get value from your product. So it's a lot easier to create value and to help more people out if you charge higher prices. It's easier to sell. So that's one thing. And trust me, right from the very beginning, like I said, I was selling right from $2,000 for $225 per quarter for three months subscription. So I started right from the very beginning. We also charged, I think it was $700 a year. So you could buy $225 per quarter or $700 per year. So right from the beginning, I started charging high prices and I have to admit it was basically I had a partner who insisted we charge high prices. Otherwise, I would have chickened out and I probably would have sold it for $37 a quarter or $100 a year or something like that. Luckily, I had this partner that said, no, we're charging higher prices. So I've always charged higher prices.

## [06:03 - 09:06]

I've sold home study courses, $500, $1,000, $10,000. I've had live events at $25,000. One interesting thing with pricing, and then I'll close here on the pricing piece, is I had a coaching program. The coaching program, it was $10,000 for three months. Okay? So basically, it was, I'll just write it in here. Basically this coaching program, it was 10K, never use your fingers here. It was $10,000 for three months. And it was basically a coaching program to teach people how to do product launches. Did this long ago. I also had an $18,000 level. So people could either have, buy it, and it was the same three months, and they could either get it for $10,000 or for $18,000. Now for $18,000, they got just a little bit more of me. They got to talk to me on the phone a few times. There really wasn't all that much extra that they got. And it was almost twice the price. $18,000 for three months, that's $6,000 a month, that's a lot of money. And they got, we had some group calls that they got. So everyone would get on these group calls. I presented, on the group calls, I would present information that went on for whatever, eight weeks. In fact, you know what, it wasn't even three months. It was, I think it was two months long. And they got some one-on-one calls. And these people got a few extra one-on-one calls. So a funny thing happened. 25% of the people bought this level. We called it the concierge level. It completely shocked me. So if you do the math, let's just say that we had 20 people. Many people in 15 bought at $10,000. So that's $150,000. And we had five people bought by at $18,000. And so what is that? That would be $50,000 plus that would be $90,000. And these numbers are actually from memory. They're sort of close to what I ended up doing. So we did $240,000. Now one of the things you're going to notice, if I had 20 people who had bought at, you know, if I didn't have this higher level, if I just, if I didn't have this higher level, if I just had the $10,000 level, then we would have had 20 people buy at $10,000. And that would have been $200,000. So right off the bat, I went from, instead of doing $200,000, I did $240,000.

## [09:06 - 11:47]

That's what an extra 20%, I mean 20% more money. And I did it just by, you know, making up this level. Just by saying, you know, here's this extra level and you can get more calls from me. It just wasn't a very big deal. And I got 20% and extra $40,000. So first lesson is the math worked pretty well for me. But the thing I really wanted to get at was, of the people who took the concierge level, every single one of them went on to do a launch and have great success. The hit rate up here, several of these people, maybe half of those people actually did a launch and half the people actually didn't do the launch. So I just got more driven people. These people were more driven and they took more action. Did they take more action because they spent that money or did they spend that money because they were the kind of people that take action? I don't know. You'll never know something like that. But I'll tell you something else about those people. Of those people at the concierge level, all of them, I think, maybe almost all of them, maybe one didn't actually went on to become friends with me. They're people I stay in touch with till this day. I've gotten to know. I went back and I asked them, why did they spend that extra money? And really, it was they wanted to get on my radar. They wanted me to know that they were serious. So there's a lot of dynamics that go into charging higher prices. And I'm not saying you have to charge $18,000 or you have to charge $1,000 or you have to charge $500 or even $100. But if it's the learned guitar market, then the products are anywhere from $17 to $300. If it's the make money online market, it's anywhere from $7 up to $10,000 or $20,000. It varies by what your market is. If it's the dating market, it's a whole different dynamic. If it's the knitting market, it's a whole different dynamic. So what your actual prices will be will depend on your market. But what I'm saying is you don't want to be at the low end of your market. You want to be closer to the high end of your market. You don't want to compete on price. You want to compete on value. Did I create massive value for these people? You bet I did. The people that had launches, they got massive value. These people that paid the $18,000, they got even more value because all of them did launches. And they went on to create this ongoing relationship with me. So deliver value, compete on value, don't compete on pricey.
